item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image originates from here, and belongs to user Corrine. As there is no indication it was released under creative commons, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, spiders can survive explosive plane crashes?

Jesus…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rediscovered this while using the Random SCP button, and it's still great. I think this was actually part of the inspiration for me writing SCP-3095.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clouds? Not scary.

Spiders? Kinda scary.

Spider clouds? Absolutely horrifying.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just wanna say that I'm not even scared of spiders. That being said, Jesus, even my skin crawled. Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Stasis locks". The Foundation has the technology to freeze time in large areas?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better than snakes on a plane… SPOIDERS!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly i'd like it better if they could be destroyed. as it is this just seems like an arachnophobes nightmare. The agent log was terrifying, but the spiders being able to survive a plane crash and fiery explosion ruined it for me. -1

also shouldnt it be called aerial arachnids? Since, you know, there are millions of them and not just one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IT'S THE WORLD WIDE WEB.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the spider cloud and all. Plane ending sort of feels off to me though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was…a whole heck of a lot better than the first time I read it when it was first posted. Yikes. My only quibble is that they really freaked out about spiders on the plane.

That said, I can kind of understand it. Millions of spiders crawling around in what was supposed to be an airtight fuselage…*shudder*. And the sheer number of the spiders had to drastically increase the consumption rate.

Definitely a solid +1 now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Welp, plane is full of spiders. Time to crash and burn."
This is the only correct response to the situation and the flight crew should get medals.

Minor technical quibble: Fuel-air explosives do not work well in reduced atmospheric pressure. I'm not enough of a weapons tech guy to say for sure, but I think that they would not be ideal at 30-40K feet. A FAE would, however, be a better choice for procedure C. At feeding altitude there should be no diminished impact and the negative pressure created in the wake of the blast wave is as close as modern tech can get to a 100% guaranteed sterile kill zone. Without the concern of lingering damage to the ecosystem that an uber-poison carries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat idea, decent execution and there are some nice touches, like the researcher coldly observing that

Agent R. was in radio contact for a further 18 hours and 7 minutes. During this time Agent R. was unable to provide more useful information on the nature of SCP-1506

, the energy drink thing and the cockpit transcript.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thought of spiders that can get you at any time or place is something I'd rather not have. But in lieu of that, everyone else can have it too

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spiders. Why'd it have to be spiders. Flying spiders, no less.

This is seirously your first SCP? and it's a Keter? I applaud you, sir; this is far better then I could have ever expected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Any animalia"
Shouldn't this be 'Any species of animal in the kingdom animalia' or something along those lines?
Apart from that, looks really good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has now had a very major revision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I thought this was a bit meh, I liked spiders so this seemed just a bit meh but after driving to work and seeing similar clouds it actually freaked me out a bit. I think the getting snatched up by dangling webs bit creeps me out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In hindsight I'm not sure why I said that since I've upvoted three articles that where later deleted.
Lapse in sanity, I say! Andtotallynotjustmebeingdumbhonest

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At that point why waste a D-class, just strap stuff to a cow. More efficient resource expenditure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. It's a mildly novel idea and that's about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My apologies if I missed something in one of your posts somewhere, I'm too busy balling 'til I fall.

You probably didn't use any hashtags though so I think I come out on top here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find it amoosing that moose is the only one to even touch upon how many, many ecosystems would get #REKT because spiders help regulate the insect population.

ahem

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it amoosing that moose is the only one to even touch upon how many, many ecosystems would get #REKT because spiders help regulate the insect population. The Foundation leading a campaign to eradicate spiders would not only be ridiculously difficult and normalcy-disrupting, it would be downright suicidal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The real take-away from this discussion is that most of you guys are incredibly patronizing about the concept of "villagers", as if they were some sort of magic beast…I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are thousands of governmental organizations in the US alone with the sole mission of mosquito abatement.

Thousands, huh? Can you point us to a list or something? Besides, the overall level of funding is what is important, not the number of organizations, as an organization might be very minor and have little personnel or funding. Look at the money the US has put into killing Iraqis over the last decade and compare with the amount spent on eradicating mosquitoes or mosquito-borne diseases.

A lot of the time, those stagnant pools of water happen to be wetland systems, stormwater infrastructure, and other things that people would be kind of pissed about if you got rid of them.

Nevertheless, developed countries, even those in the tropics, seem to deal with mosquito populations more effectively than places like the Congo etc. Again, according to the CDC (talking about Malaria specifically):

"An estimated 91% of deaths in 2010 were in the African Region, followed by the South-East Asian Region (6%), and the Eastern Mediterranean Region (3%). About 86% of deaths globally were in children". That's 99% of malaria deaths globally in Africa, SE Asia and the Eastern Mediterranean (i.e. less developed parts of the world). No doubt part of that is due to these regions being tropical, but drill down and see which countries have the biggest problem and I'll bet you it is the less developed ones in those regions.
Source: http://www.cdc.gov/malaria/about/facts.html

In the US, diseases of popular concern that have mosquito vectors are West Nile virus and encephalitis. There aren't vaccines for these diseases.

Here's part of what the US CDC has to say about WNV: "Fortunately, most people infected with WNV will have no symptoms. About 1 in 5 people who are infected will develop a fever with other symptoms. Less than 1% of infected people develop a serious, sometimes fatal, neurologic illness."
Source: http://www.cdc.gov/westnile/index.html

Total number of cases reported in the US in 2012: 5674. (from a report accessible from the CDC page). So, less than 1% means < 57 for the entire US over a whole year.

There's data on various kinds of encephalitis on related CDC pages. Bottom line, a drop in the bucket of fatalities in the US compared with obesity-related disease, gun deaths, motor vehicle deaths etc etc etc.

As for predictions related to climate change theories - yeah, let's wait and see. These predictions all rely on various simplifying assumptions that the authors don't go out of their way to tell you about.

Anyway, this is getting way off topic. I still see no reason the Foundation would have to come out of the shadows because of this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All I could think once I realized that they were flying spiders was, "NOPE NOPE NOPE NOPE NOPE"

Very, very good idea. I hate spiders; I was shifting uncomfortably the entire time I read it. This is what an SCP should look like. Well thought-out, well written, and well received. Great job.

Obviously I gave it an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Only just read this. Super creepy. Gives me the heebie jeebies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With the changes you've made, this merits a solid upvote.

We're going to need nerds.

Yes. This is good. I am pleased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and the developed world just isn't trying all that hard to deal with the problem.

There are thousands of governmental organizations in the US alone with the sole mission of mosquito abatement.

Mosquitos can be dealt with by denying them the stagnant pools of water they need to breed

A lot of the time, those stagnant pools of water happen to be wetland systems, stormwater infrastructure, and other things that people would be kind of pissed about if you got rid of them.

Also, availablility of vaccines and other medical care etc.

In the US, diseases of popular concern that have mosquito vectors are West Nile virus and encephalitis. There aren't vaccines for these diseases. As climate change moves forward, the developed world will increasingly be dealing with malaria and dengue fever, also diseases with no vaccines available.

Just sayin'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because I feel personally stupid upvoting something that will be removed.

This really isn't cool. Please don't operate like this. Remember, you're supposed to vote based on article content. It's true that you have the right to not vote for any reason you like, but this reason still ain't cool.

I mean, how will articles survive without upvotes? That's the only way they can survive, by definition. If too many people operate this way, articles will eventually start falling by the wayside for silly reasons.

Also, I guarantee you every veteran and staff member on this site has upvoted many articles that were later deleted, including myself. Honestly this is a mark of pride, it really is. Vote based on what you think, not the direction you think things are going to go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is missing the key point that the spider clouds are anomalous. There's likely many reasons to believe that the spider clouds thing going public will also lead to other anomalous things being discovered and going public as well.

Plus, eradicating spiders would destroy normalcy, for all the reasons Voct pointed out and more. The Foundation exists to preserve normalcy. They would never even try to eradicate all spiders, not without a bigger reason — and if that reason existed, they would ALREADY be trying, which would firmly place the SCP in a world that is not our world, which typically SCP articles try to avoid.

The Foundation doesn't want humanity to be at the mercy of supernatural forces. Spiders occupy central roles in many areas of our culture and our lives (the aspect of everyone being afraid of them is still central). "Spiders exist" is central to our normal lives. Destroying all spiders to stop a supernatural force, however dangerous, would go completely against the central mission of the Foundation.

The mosquitos thing is totally different because it had nothing to do with anomalous forces… that we know of. (Hey, there's an -EX idea for ya!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmmmmm. I'm really not sure how I feel about this. I really want to like it, but it's not working yet. The concept is good, just the mention of the plane incident is horrifying, and the fact that it's so widespread (and any spiders can be incorporated, not just some anomalous subspecies) feels pretty scary.

But still…

Well, let's pick out what I can pick out.

as instances are far too aggressive to have their web structure examined internally.

Nonsense. Aggressive animals and insects are studied well enough in real life by knocking them out or killing them. This feels like a contrivance so that the author doesn't have to explain those bits.

Modelling shows the whiplash effect from this will kill human prey 50% of the time.

The whiplash thing seems a bit too comical for me. The mental image I get is cartoonish.

Observation indicates that humans are the preferred prey of SCP-1506-1

Missed period. Also, why? Just to be even more specially scary? Not really buying it.

Finally, the dialogue in the interview feels forced; it doesn't read like real people talking. I did like the "We're going to need nerds" line, but the rest doesn't work. (The next closest thing to working is the killer low-flying weather balloon line, but that feels a bit forced as well.)

You also have a number of errors remaining, like "it's" in the final paragraph and the missing period mentioned above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure that the mosquito case is all that relevant. Most of those deaths occur in the developing world and the developed world just isn't trying all that hard to deal with the problem. Mosquitos can be dealt with by denying them the stagnant pools of water they need to breed and that's more likely to happen in a developed country. Also, availablility of vaccines and other medical care etc.

Anyway, I have my doubts about whether spiders could actually be eradicated, and I definitely think that their eradication would lead to other huge problems but again, the premise here is that they represent such a threat that the decision has been made to do it. If it can be done at all (big if), i think it can be done by the methods I mentioned.

And the argument is actually about whether the Foundation would have to go public in this scenario, not whether eradicating spiders would be feasible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mosquitoes kill far more people each year through bloodborne illness, are much more reviled in the public eye, and have been subject to many attempts to negate or dampen their harm.
Look how well that's worked.
Doing that with the entirety of spiders is even more ridiculously unfeasible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh, you're making a lot of unwarranted asumptions there. I haven't actually eaten a spider myself, but I've certainly seen them being sold as food with my own eyes and have eaten other things being sold by the same vendor (various kinds of insect). Given my proclivity for travelling in what most of us here would regard as exotic corners of the world, it's probably just a matter of time until I try spider.

Also, I'm familiar with the ecological and economic value of spiders; personally, I think if we really ever got to the point of having to eradicate spiders, it would be a lose-lose scenario for humanity. BUT, playing along with the scenario we are discussing, the decision has been made to eradicate spiders because in the Foundationverse they now represent a terrible threat that they don't in our reality. The only questions are (1) how will the spiders be eradicated? and (2) whether the Foundation has to come out and void everything it exists for by telling everyone about the darkness the Foundation exists to hide, or not.

As far as (1) is concerned, the huge huge number of spiders necessitates a top-down response. Notwithstanding the Chinese government's quite devasting campaigns against the sparrow and other pests, it's not going to be accomplished by villagers swatting spiders with their…I dunno, do villagers have newspapers? Point is, it'll probably have to be done by a mass extermination method such as spidercide sprayed from aircraft, or some kind of biological weapon such as a virus that attacks spiders. This is a direct consequence of the very point you bring up: the humongous numbers of the things.

Anyway, point is, it wouldn't necessarily need the cooperation of people on the ground. Once the various world governments are on board, it would just be a matter of throwing money at them and giving them the tools they need to get the job done. And if it did require the cooperation of the villagers? Well, give them money to kill spiders, and they'll kill spiders.

As for (2), see, here's the thing: there might be eleventy-million-zillion to the power of googleplex spiders on Earth but there are fewer than 200 national governments. We can assume that the governments of world powers will be briefed secretly by the Foundation and will on board with the eradication program. Most of the rest are heavily dependent on foreign aid from those powers and they'll come on board if the powers unite in threatening to turn off the money fountain. Any dissenting governments, given the vital nature of the program, will be subject to immense diplomatic pressure. Those villagers of yours won't even get a say.

Also, I'm sure it wouldn't be beyond the ingenuity of humanity to capture part of a spider cloud and show on film that it was indeed spiders who were snatching people from the face of the Earth. Big propaganda win that would convince many doubters of the need to take drastic measures. Sure, in the developed world you'd get some hippies staging "Save the spider" rallies and hugging spiders but most people aren't going to worry about spiders in the way they do about whales and pandas and other photogenic animals. I mean, most people in the developed world don't hesitate to kill a spider if they find one in their homes. And in the undeveloped world? Most governments have a habit of just doing what they want. They probably don't even need a propaganda campaign, no matter how delicious the locals think spiders are.

Given the premise that the Foundation's work in keeping the anomalous world a secret is vitally important for the good of humanity (a core premise underwriting the existence of the Foundation), it makes absolutely no sense for the Foundation to blow that off and go public. Then you would have humanity dealing with the spiders AND stepping back into the darkness - the very darkness we are trying to protect them from. Even if you dealt with the spider problem, you would be left with the very situation that the Foundation exists to stave off. And, as I have shown, if it is possible to deal with the spider menace at all, it is possible to deal with it without taking that drastic step.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Always look up in case of SCP-1506.

Never look up in case of SCP-312.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm making it policy to always check back in on articles that I -1'd, to make sure I that I change my vote if the article has improved.

This SCP is leaps and bounds better than the original draft. +1. Those 2 point swings are nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Consider it a response to Sirius Moonlight, and to anyone who doesn't understand why total containment would require going public.

Plus sometimes you just feel like writing, y'know?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only disappointment here is that you didn't name the MTF "Peter Parker's Power Pack of Pest Patrolmen."

Other than that, I liked it, and only have one further comment…

"It's rainin' spiders, hallelu-OHGODWHY"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't tell how serious you thought I was being, but I assure you it was not at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… think for a minute, okay? Overcome your own personal visceral dislike for spiders, stop attributing it to all people everywhere, and actually think. There's at least 30,000 species of spider known (different websites give different figures, but the lowest I've seen is 30,000). They live on every continent except Antarctica. They live on every island, regardless of whether the island is large enough to support humans. They live in the hottest jungles, the muckiest swamps, the driest deserts, the highest mountains, the deepest caves. In empty houses, and deserted factories, and abandoned cars, and in every hidden corner of every landfill and forest and park and beach and subway station and church steeple and field and garden, and even in ponds and lakes and rivers and streams and brooks and creeks and lagoons.

There are literally trillions of spiders on this planet. I've seen one estimate on the order of 3 * 1016.

And not all cultures are arachnophobic. There are animistic cultures that deify spiders. Not everyone is going to be willing to go along with what the Big City Government says, especially if it makes a mockery of the Old Ways. If you think it's tough getting all the villagers to bring all their children to be vaccinated, try convincing them that they have to either capture or kill all spiders everywhere. Even if the villagers have just seen one of their own children being hauled up into the sky and eaten by a weird white cloud, try telling them that THE CLOUD WAS MADE OF SPIDERS AND AN INDIVIDUAL SPIDER COULD BE ANYWHERE and therefore they need to burn all their own crops and trees and homes and temples and schools and hospitals. What happens if all you can show them is a video of the cloud catching a foreigner on the other side of the world?

What about agriculture ? Spiders catch insects that eat crops!

What about insect-borne diseases ? Spiders catch insects that spread diseases!

What about the various industrial and scientific uses of spider silk? What about all the insects and birds and fish that eat spiders as a key part of their diet? Hell, what about people who eat spiders as a delicacy? Make spider-eating illegal and you'll just drive up the price and incentivize secret backyard spider farms.

If the Foundation were to go public, to blow the walls off modern science and consensus reality, to actually show that there truly is weird-ass shit going on, shit which makes no sense and is supposed to be completely impossible but is happening anyway… then you would have a better chance of convincing billions of people that if the Foundation says to do something stupid like "eradicate or capture all spiders everywhere", it's for a damn good reason and you should damn well do your best to cooperate. Not all people, though. Not by a long shot.

The smallpox virus was only one (or possibly two) species. It could only truly live within the bodies of unvaccinated humans. People were able to see the effects of smallpox outbreaks with their own eyes, were able to see that the vaccinated children escaped the hideous scars which permanently disfigured the luckier smallpox victims. Smallpox caused no significant ecological or economic benefits; in fact, worldwide it killed millions of people per year.1

The eradication of smallpox was a huge concerted multinational effort that took over a century and a half of constant effort, starting during the Napoleonic Wars and lasting all the way to the later stages of the Cold War. And that involved total public knowledge of what the problem actually was.

Try doing this secretly, and about "all spiders everywhere", because "sometimes they form a cloud that goes into the sky and catches a person and eats him".

Footnotes
1. No wisecracks about how the death of millions of humans per year is an ecological and/or economic benefit in and of itself, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think it would be hard to get the public to go for it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, once these things start picking off their preferred prey - i.e. humans - in numbers, the populations of the various countries will demand that their governments declare war on spiders. Any government that wants to stay in power will have a hard time resisting.

Second, where do you think the Foundation gets its funding from? From the world's governments, that's where. Funding the Foundation to make war on spiders is just a short step away from the governments doing it directly.

Third, constructing a public relations campaign to support the war would be money for jam for whichever agencies got the job: It's a war on spiders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you, I'm taking this on board. As I've said elsewhere, I'm planning a major revision now. I did release this far too early, I see in retrospect. I won't make a habit of that. Some of the stuff you dislike was added specifically to answer questions people had when I was showing my sandbox in the chat. I preferred less information for more "oh god spiders could eat me at any moment" horror.

The dialogue was written on the run in regards to criticism I had after it was first posted but I disagree with your opinion, even reading aloud. It feels to me more like a conversation between people who know each other pretty well. I'm not biased or anything… The bit I dislike most is the last line as it's a bit "beeeeeeeeeeeees" but the information it has is pretty much the point of that log, heat, prey restriction and the idea of spiders crawling all over you. I don't know how to present that better within that kind of log. With a contained instance, that information should be covered with test logs and the conversation obsolete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why? Couldn't the Foundation remain in the shadows and operate through the various world governments (as I understand it does on other matters)?

… do you have any idea what it would take to convince all world governments to declare war on spiders ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To even get close to accomplishing that — assuming the Foundation wants to try — the Foundation would have to go public.

Why? Couldn't the Foundation remain in the shadows and operate through the various world governments (as I understand it does on other matters)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some people would say that using a fuel air explosive to detonate a spider colony extreme.

Not I sir, not I.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as the Foundation can tell, any and all spiders can become involved in this. Anywhere you have two spiders together, they could somehow be contributing to the formation of 1506. Ultimate total containment could, hypothetically, require the containment of all spiders everywhere on the planet, which at the very least would devastate all ecosystems. To even get close to accomplishing that — assuming the Foundation wants to try — the Foundation would have to go public.

That's was meant to part of the unsaid conclusion.

I'm planning a major revision involving containment of at least one instance

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could send a D-class in, with a whole bunch of robust cameras/sensors/etc attached to him/her?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Today's forecast : cloudy with a chance of spiders.
Take your upvote, you horrible person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd upvote the shit out of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Points for creativity, but this has systemic execution problems. There are other kinds of lock porn besides that which immediately concerns containment cells, and this is definitely in that territory. Like others, I don't care for a blackboxed SCP being necessary to containment for no apparent reason, there's a lot of unnecessary redaction, and the dialogue between the agents made me wince.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because I feel personally stupid upvoting something that will be removed.
Though, I'm going to upvote anyway as I like the concept of flying spiders rockin' little lift-cheuttes into the air.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love you both for these posts.
May they be upvoted in spirit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is also plenty of braces at the Foundation's disposal to avoid injury at those break-neck speeds.

Even if you keep the neck supported, organs will still slam into their respective containers with enough speed to cause fatal injury. Your brain will slam into your skull, the major veins/arteries connecting to your heart will tear because it just slammed into your ribcage at high speeds, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … if you're gonna upvote if it sticks around, why not just upvote now? It's not as though you lose rep for supporting failed articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[REDACTED]
Nothing I said the other fellas above hadn't said. So. Good luck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the biggest problem is that there is no containment. The SCP object usually ends up destroyed, and when it isn't it is just repelled so that it can be destroyed later. The Foundation could study these colonies and look for any kind of unusual influence that may have hold over the spiders. It's freaky enough, and has potential, but I think that it can use some serious work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"All in favor of giving our full and open support to the shadowy paragovernment cabal of questionable ethics say aye."

"Let the records show that the 'Fuck Those Fucking Creepy Ass Spiders, Just Fucking Lock Them Up' treaty has been unanimously passed."
